course_description: Economics focuses on choices between alternative ways of using resources and the consequences of these choices, which often have to be made in the face of problems associated with the availability of information and limitations to knowledge. These choices concern issues such as what to consume, what to produce, how to produce it, how to organize production between different economic entities, and what prices to charge. Choices taken by individuals need to be coordinated to avoid wasted opportunities, the more so the more that specialization is taking place at the level of the individual, firm or national economy. A major coordinating role is played by price mechanisms, i.e. adjustments in relative prices and the incentives provided by profits and losses. However, much coordination is achieved via the use of planning and institutions. Even with coordination assisted by these devices coordination failures can still occur, such as major slumps in employment and international trade. Economic theory can help to assess when these problems are likely and how they may be dealt with.

requirements.incompatible: ECON2010, or 2400 or 7003 or 7010 or 7011 or MEBS7103
requirements.assumed_background: No background in economics is assumed. However, if you want to do some reading before the course starts so as to get a feel for the economic way of thinking, then a good source for microeconomics would be Tim Hardford (2006) The Undercover Economist , Oxford, Oxford University Press. For macroeconomics, perhaps try Michael Stewart, Keynes and After (various editions), Harmondsworth, Penguin.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: To provide students with the economic principles underpinning much thinking in other management disciplines (such as marketing and strategic management). To demonstrate why managers might benefit from a sound knowledge and ability to apply economic analysis and techniques. To help students move away from a simplified black and white way of thinking about economics, and move more towards seeing issues from different perspectives so as to make commitments using well argued reasoning. To develop skills in identifying which economic tools may be useful in particular situations and then applying these tools to analyse issues businesses face.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify situations where economic theories will assist in arriving at well reasoned decisions.
Employ the knowledge of a variety of economic concepts to deal with resource allocation problems.
Apply economic theories to analyse rather than simply describe economic behaviour.
Critically evaluate choices made by individuals and firms using economic concepts.

assessment_details.title: Individual Newspaper Article Reviews
assessment_details.task_description:
Newspaper Article 1 (15%).

By referring to any Australian or International newspapers, find one article that relates to at least one microeconomic concept discussed in Lectures 1, 2, 3, or 4. This newspaper article must be written in English and published after 1 January, 2012 . You may refer to online newspaper websites to obtain your article. Radio and TV transcripts should not to be used. Your selected newspaper article must satisfy these conditions in order to pass this piece of assessment.

Using 500 to 750 words (with 12 point font, double line spacing, Times New Roman font, and 2.5cm margins), address each of the following criteria.

i) Briefly summarise the overall basis of the article in a few sentences.

ii) Clearly state the microeconomic concept(s) that forms the basis of the article.

iii) Critically analyse your article, making reference to the microeconomic concept(s) you have stated. (Hint: by reflecting on perceived strengths and weaknesses in the article, assess how well you believe the article communicates an economic understanding to the reader).

iv) Using your analysis in iii), give an example of another real business (different to any mentioned in the article) that could be affected by the microeconomic concept(s) covered in the article. Briefly justify why this would be so.

v) If you were the manager of the real business you selected in iv), make at least one recommendation you would implement in this selected business based on the analysis of your newspaper article.

vi) Make a brief statement regarding what a business manager could learn from analysing this newspaper article.

vii) Scan (onto an A4 page) the article you have reviewed. Ensure the name of the newspaper, date published , and page number are on this photocopied article (all hand written if need be).

Note: Your first article may be submitted by Friday 13 April at 9am using material covered in lectures 1 to 4. It will be marked over the weekend, and returned with feedback ideally by Monday 16 April by 12 noon. Students are encouraged to submit the first article by 13 April in order to benefit from any feedback for their second newspaper article. If this early submission option is not taken, both articles are to be submitted by the due date.

Newspaper Article 2 (15%).

By referring to any Australian or International newspapers, find one article that relates to at least one microeconomic concept discussed in Lectures 1 to 6 inclusively. This article must be written in English and published after 1 January, 2012 . You may refer to online newspaper websites to obtain your article. Radio and TV transcripts should not to be used. Your selected newspaper article must satisfy these conditions in order to pass this piece of assessment.

Using 500 to 750 words (with 12 point font, double line spacing, Times New Roman font, and 2.5cm margins), cover points i) to vii) as was required for Newspaper Article 1.

IMPORTANT : Please ensure that each article submission is compiled as follows:

1. First page is your signed Economic Assignment Cover Sheet.

2. Second page is your clearly scanned newspaper article (referenced and able to be easily read).

3. Third page is the marking guide, with your name indicated, and a box ticked to indicate if it is article 1 or 2.

4. Your written work.

If you submit two articles at the same time (ie: the early feedback option is not taken), please ensure that two separate files are submitted, one file for each article.

assessment_details.title: Case Analysis
assessment_details.task_description:
Group Presentation.

Students are required to form small groups of up to five students. The presentation is to be 20 to 25 minutes in length. The topic, date and time for each presentation will be allocated in the first tutorial on Day 1. Note : final group sizes and length of presentation will depend on actual final enrolment numbers. Details indicated here may vary and will be finalised on Day 1.

Each group will be allocated, and required to focus on, one of the following companies listed on the Australian Stock Exchange: Cochlear (COH), Billabong (BBG), Energy Resources Australia of Australia (ERA), Sigma Pharmaceuticals (SIP), and Select Harvests (SHV). Students are expected to access information from company websites, the ASX website, news articles, or other similarly available sources. Students should not contact or approach any company directly for information. The specific requirements of the group presentation are summarised in the Group Presentation Marking Guide. This guide is included with this course profile document (see attached link below). Students are expected to address each of the criteria in their presentation, so please refer to this Marking Guide carefully.

The specific requirements of the Group Presentation are:

1. Introduction and brief overview of your allocated company and its operations.

2. State the key economic theories and concepts your presentation will focus on. These should be the central theme of your presentation. In all cases, microeconomic theory should form the basis of your analysis. However, some macroeconomic concepts can be incorporate where relevant (by reading ahead of the lectures if need be).

3. Analyse your companys current performance and their chosen strategic direction. Highlight the specific choices the company has made. Assess the logic of these strategic choices (considering the environment, available resources, and existing market conditions). It should be clear by this stage in your presentation as to why you selected nominated the key economic theories at the start of your presentation. Demonstration of these economic theories and concepts should be clear through practical applications relevant to your company.

4. Fr om your analysis, identify current strategic issues the company is facing. Suggest possible options and choices managers could adopt to address these issues. Indicated what choice(s) you would recommend to the CEO and board of the company to address these issues. Focus on your key economic theories and concepts to support your recommendations.

After the presentation concludes, your group must then have one or two well thought out questions to promote class discussion and/or debate. The group (or nominated member) is required to lead a class discussion for an expected 10 15 minutes (or longer if the class is willing). The discussion should aim to expand the theoretical concepts explored in the presentation. To conclude this discussion, an overall assessment by your group should be made as to the practicalities and limitations of applying selected economic theories to your allocated company.

assessment_details.title: Individual Essay
assessment_details.task_description:
Major Assignment .

The focus of this course is to better understand economic concepts so as to then apply them in business situations. This, in part, will require interventions at times by managers and choices to be made in order to coordinate information and knowledge to avoid wasted opportunities. The course also highlights the importance of understanding the roles consumption, investment, and government actions play in taking business decisions, coordinating activities, and assessing strategic directions.

Economic theories and concepts used in this course have examined how and why businesses and markets work the way they do. With this knowledge, students have been given various tools to assist them in making more informed, strategic business decisions.

Using the perspectives outlined above, write an essay reflecting on a variety of economic concepts that impact your organisation (where you are currently working, or have worked in the past). The essay must address the following points.

1. Provide a brief background of your organisation.

2. Outline the key economic concepts you believe are relevant to your organisation.

3. Critically analyse aspects of your organisation that demonstrate your understanding of the key economic concepts you have identified. The analysis should reflect on:

i) Possible past successes or strengths, failures or weaknesses observed in your organisation.

ii) Why successful and unsuccessful outcomes occurred.

iii) What options could be taken to reinforce any perceived/real strengths, or to rectify any weaknesses.

Note: Please be sure to use analysis, incorporating economics concepts. Dont just describe events and outcomes .

4. Based on your analysis, what recommendation(s) would you suggest your organisation implements? Be sure these recommendations are practical with any implementation problems/issues discussed and addressed.

5. A conclusion reviewing the key elements and findings of the essay.

NOTE: The essay is to be between 2,500 and 3,000 words (12 point font, Times New Roman, double line spacing, 2.5 cm margins). Please note that words exceeding the 3000 limit will not be marked. Appropriate headings should be used in the essay that guides the reader.

IMPORTANT : Please ensure that your submission is compiled as follows:

1. First page is a your signed Economic Assignment Cover Sheet

2. Second page is the marking guide with your name indicated.

3. Your written work.

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Introduction and Setting the Scene. (Lecture): Examine the linkages between economic theories and strategic management concepts. Introduce the notion of economic analysis as being one of various tools available to make strategic business decisions. Outline core economic concepts including the cost benefit principle, decision making pitfalls, opportunity cost, marginal analysis, and economic surplus. Chapter 1 and Chapter 2. Readings: Barney (1991), Porter (2008), Whittington (2001).
Tutorial 1 (Tutorial)

What price do we charge? Part 1. (Lecture): Understanding supply and demand, using supply and demand models in business decision making, predicting likely market responses to different choices and strategies ensuring efficient uses of resources. Chapter 3 and Chapter 5 (p137). Readings: Peteraf (1993).
Tutorial 2 (Tutorial)

What price do we charge? Part 2. (Lecture): The concept of elasticity of demand and supply. Examine the application and implications of elasticity theory for business managers and pricing strategies. Chapter 4.
Tutorial 3 (Tutorial)

Economic Efficiency and Market Failure. (Lecture): Defining key terms such as producer and consumer surplus, maximising the surplus, and dead weight loss. Assessing the impacts on free markets where governments intervene. Examine price ceilings, price floors, black-markets, impacts of subsidies, quotas, and import tariffs, and the implications for businesses. Chapter 5.
Tutorial 4 (Tutorial)

Profit Maximising using Microeconomic Theory. (Lecture): Assess short run and long run productions costs of a firm, Total Cost, Average Cost, Marginal Cost. Introduce the theory of Perfect Competition as a basis for analysing more realistic market structures. Examine a perfectly competitive firms profit maximising output, loss minimising and shutdown situations. Chapter 6 and Chapter 7. Readings: Richardson (1999)
Tutorial 5 (Tutorial)

Monopolies, Cartels and the ACCC. (Lecture): How monopolies emerge, how a monopoly maximises profit, real world applications of the theory, the implications to society from both economic and business perspectives, the role of the ACCC and the economic theory behind their decisions, cartel behavior and some recent examples. Chapter 8. Readings: Davies and Davies (1984)
Tutorial 6 (Tutorial)

Monopolistic Markets. (Lecture): Monopolistic firms, their business strategies and characteristics, profit maximising criteria, comparisons to other market structures and implication for strategic decision making. Chapter 9. Readings: Markides and Williamson (1994), Peteraf (1993)
Guest Lecture - Microeconomics (Guest Lecture)

Introduction to Macro Economics. (Lecture): Introduction to Macro Economics. Key concepts including the Circular Flow of Funds Model for GDP, economic growth and the Solow Growth Model (plus endogenous growth ideas), understanding inflation and the CPI, and models to understand aspects of unemployment. Chapter 12 and Chapter 14. Readings: Odlyzko (2004), Radford (1945), Solow (1994)
Group presentation preparation and consultation (all groups)
Group presentation preparation and consultation (all groups)

Aggregate Demand and Aggregate Supply (AD-AS). (Lecture): Establish linkages between various economic variables using the AD-AS Model. Review reasons for shifts in aggregate demand and supply, and using the AD-AS model consider implications of various macroeconomic events. Show how the model is applied to predict real world possible outcomes from specific policy actions. Chapter 13.
Group Presentations (two groups)

Fiscal Policy. (Lecture): Defining and applying Fiscal Policy. Government deficits and surplus, levels of debt, crowding out private investment. Understanding the various policy measures governments take to stabilise economies during periods of boom and bust. Applying theoretical models to help analyse fiscal policy decisions. Adopt the models to provide a framework to appreciate the ongoing political debates about an economy. Examine how to apply the models to make more informed strategic business decisions. Chapter 17.
Group presentation preparation and consultation (all groups)
Group Presentations (three groups)
Overview of Course Concepts (Review): Review of student assignment findings using economic analysis. General concluding comments on the applicability of course theories for business managers.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

Definition of Plagiarism

Examples of plagiarism as given in the Universitys Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Offering an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writers work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as ones own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the Universitys Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those follow-up papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide.

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland's Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University's central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers a range of economic theories and concepts useful in the business environment. With no prior knowledge of economics, it aims to provide students with a solid grounding in fundamental economic theories relevant for business managers. In the first lecture, the linkage between economic theories and strategic business decisions is explored. This linkage then forms an underlying theme throughout the course. This theme is developed not only in lectures, but also through student activities including reviewing current newspaper articles, in-class case presentations, and writing a reflective essay on their current work organization. These activities are all aimed at developing an understanding of economic theories and how they can strategically and practically be applied in business environments. The early lectures introduce fundamental terminology and concepts in economics, with an emphasis on price setting, profit maximization, costs, and differing market structures. Later, problems in allocating available resources with limited information and knowledge is discussed. An appreciation of linkages between economic concepts and business disciplines such as marketing, finance, and human resource management should also become evident through the course. Having assessed organizations from a microeconomic perspective, the course then concludes by considering how organizations fit into the bigger macroeconomic picture. In the last lectures, the course focus is on GDP, growth, inflation, unemployment, aggregate demand and supply, fiscal policy, and their relevance to business managers and decision making.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Student feedback from previous offerings of this course has been positive. The general structure of the course has not changed from last year.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0-19.99%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20-44.99%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

A common cause of students getting this grade on an answer, aside from having done little work or achieved a poor understanding, is a failure to answer the question that was set (for example, if the question says 'explore the relationships between the following concepts', you must explore the relationships between them, not define or describe them), or a failure to attempt all parts of the question. So, read the wording very carefully. 45-49.99%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic requirement of being able to think like an economist is demonstrating that you consider the relative costs and benefits of alternative possible choices and the implications of differences in the incentives they offer to decision makers. If you fail to do this, you may have a hard time getting a 4, let alone a better grade, even if you look at the pros and cons of a particular choice from the standpoint of relevant economic theory. 50-64.99%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Note here the phrase 'ability to apply': if your answers primarily report on facts that you have gathered or merely summarize the work of others you will be failing to show what you can do with economic concepts as tools of analysis and you will not score more than a 4. For a 5 or beyond, you must show that you can think like an economist rather than merely displaying abilities in terms of researching facts or what some economists think. 65-74.99%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

To get beyond a 5, it is helpful to be able to demonstrate that you know what is predicted by relevant economic theory in the context in question and consider how well the predictions match up with facts. 75-84.99%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Integrative thinking that relates concepts from different parts of the course will be highly regarded as will the application of ideas/arguments from reading beyond the prescribed text or lecture notes. 85-100%

Other Requirements & Comments:
late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made on the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. An extension made on medical grounds will be for the number of days for which the medical certificates indicates the student was unwell, and will not exceed 14 days. Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment, and the application form may be accessed through myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/extension-progressive-assessment ).

The penalty for late submission of an assessment item, without prior approval for an extension, will be 5% of the total mark available for the assessment item for each calendar day after the due date.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

Unless otherwise specified, ALL assignments must be lodged by the due date and time stated in the course profile, and are to be submitted through both Blackboard and Turnitin. The following should also be noted:

1. Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed Economic Assignment Cover Sheet, available online from http://www.bel.uq.edu.au/content/current_students/assignments/economics-final.pdf

2. Students must pay atttention to the order in which pages of an assignment are to be collated. Requirements for assignment submissions are under section 5.5 "Assessment Details".

3. Students must sign the assignment cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work.

4. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details.

Please also note:

· Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement.

· Incomplete or incorrect assignment coversheets can cause delays in processing your assignments.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

N/A

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

N/A
